text: That's the mammalian diving reflex! It's more pronounced in children and human groups that still do a lot of deep-sea diving, but any adult human has it to some extent. In larger doses, it helps to keep the body warm and preserve oxygen while underwater, but it's much more important for, like, otters than it is for you.
label: reasoning

text: The body tries to protect internal organs from cold shock.This involves constricting surface-layer blood vessels (so that less blood circulates near the skin) and trying to oxygenate the blood in order to support the expected higher metabolism from cells activating to increase temperature (which results in rapid breathing). It's not very effective in humans (in fact, the effect is almost entirely detrimental if you end up in cold water), but the response is almost entirely neurogenic and it's a reflex present in pretty much all mammals, so the fundamentals of it is probably a leftover from early in mammal evolution (like, 60+ million years ago) and unless you belong to a species that frequently ended up in cold water (for example if you're a polar bear or an otter) there hasn't been enough evolutionary pressure to select against it.
label: reasoning

text: Can confirm.When I learned CPR, we were told (assuming we had 2 or 3 people with us) to do CPR for no more than 2 minutes, then trade off. We had to drill the trade-off until we got that perfect - meaning I often ended up on my butt throwing myself out of the way of the next person, but we never even slowed the rhythm of chest compressions.2 minutes is exhausting, especially if you know you have just a few minutes before you have to do it again.I've spoken with EMTs who say they've been part of 10-people chains to administer CPR, and they say even that is exhausting.Keep in mind, you're kneeling over the person and pushing the top of their chest down AT LEAST a fist deep into their chest. Over and over and over (once per beat of the songs "Stayin' Alive" or "Another One Bites the Dust"). If you're not exhausted, and if you haven't broken bones, you're actively letting your patient's brain die from lack of oxygen.

text:
When I was about 9 or 10, I got lost on a hike with my dad and grandad, in the Australian bush on a 42°C day. It was a truly awful experience, what was meant to be a 1-2 hour easy bushwalk turned into over 36km of brutal marching in the heat before we found a road. We'd packed water and snacks for a short hike. All that ran out as expected. My dad, with his army training, collected some water out of a muddy puddle in a mostly dry creek bed, filtered it through a bandage out of his first aid kit, and forced me to drink it. 😭 The was kangaroo poo in the water. It was awful. I cried. But we did survive, and as far as I recall, I didn't even get sick from it. Those few disgusting sips might have saved my life!
 I never went on a Sunday hike with my family again.

text:
We don't. The other answers have it partly right: we do make some predictions. But actually the more important factor is how we adjust our movements on the fly.
There's two important brain parts in this story. There's a stripe of brain stuff running between our ears called the 'sensory motor cortex', which is the main 'moving and feeling' bit. And in the back of your brain, there's this little 'mini brain' looking thing. That's your cerebellum. It's important for fine tuning.
label: reasoning

text: They don't, at least not the way you're talking about.Our brains don't calculate how much force to use, they estimate by comparison, and then adjust for feedback.Before we try to pick something up, we normally get a chance to look at it first. Looking at an object lets our brains try to find a match to an object that is similar to what we're looking at and which we've picked up before. If we see a cubish-looking thing covered in fabric, our brains think "that looks like a pillow or a cushion, and we know how much that weighed the last time we picked one of those up". If we see a 2-liter plastic bottle filled with liquid, our brain think "OK, we've picked up bottles like this filled with liquid before, and we know how much it weighed the last time". Sometimes, our brains have extra information: "Oh, there's a cardboard box outside my door. Maybe it's the blackout curtains I ordered".
label: analogy

text: Incomplete answers in this thread. The real answer is that your brain will stimulate your muscles based on its perception of the weight of the object, recruiting a certain number of muscle fibres to lift that object but there's also a real time calculation happening as you lift.If you lift it and it's heavier than expected, e.g. a full glass that you thought was empty, you reflexively recruit more muscle fibres via a spinal cord reflex that's completely involuntary.
label: reasoning

text: You don’t calculate anything. Theres a gradient involved. When you prepare to pick it up, by touching the holdable part of the object, you haven’t exerted much force. We start at 0 joule/s. At that point, understandably, the object won’t bulge at all.Then, your brain instinctively tells you to add more energy to your upward pull (+1,+2 joules). theres a bit more reaction from the object but your brain notices it needs more energy to pull up against the Earth’s gravity so you keep adding more until you do lift it up. That’s a potential energy gradient because you keep increasing(incrementing) the energy until you get to the lift scenario.
label: reasoning

text: A particular memory or experience is encoded by a neural pathway, sometimes leading to a single neuron. Repeated experiences strengthen that pathway if they are similar enough. text: The brain had to learn it from trial and error. We're not born with that skill. Babies are terrible at picking things up. You don't have to think, the path has already been made. Your brain makes paths for each little motion and saves them for future use. And it can reuse old templates for new actions that are similar.

text: Everyone is saying prior experience plays a large part, and while this is correct (especially when dealing with the weight of an object), research also suggests that your brain uses feedback from the pads of your fingers to adjust grip pressure based on friction.Basically, when you go to pick something up, your brain adjusts how much pressure you put on it based on how much it slips against your fingertips. text: Fire is a process, it is a chemical reaction (or set of reactions) where fuel + oxygen becomes combustion products and heat is released. All of the chemicals involved are subject to gravity. Because of the heat release, the temperatures in flames are high, and flames don't exist in a vacuum, they are surrounded by air, generally much much colder air. The temperature difference means the chemicals in the flame are much lower density than the colder surrounding air, so buoyancy causes them to rise.
label: reasoning

text: I suppose if you light a fire on a white dwarf or neutron star, the flames would fall downward instead of rising upward?
label: analogy

text: This. Flames and hot gases move upward for the same reason helium balloons rise in the air or a basketball floats in water. Gravity causes more dense fluids to push up on less dense things.
label: analogy

text: Answer: Hot gas is less dense, like helium. So the heavier, cooler air around it gets pulled down and the hot fire air floats upward.
label: reasoning

text: Heat causes the air to get hotter, which makes it less dense. The surrounding air is cooler and denser, so it is pulled down due to gravity and displaces the hot air, so the net effect is the hot air rising. This is called convection.
label: reasoning

text: think about a mushroom cloud.hot stuff is going up the middle, rising up from the heat source, then cooling and falling down over the outside as it spreads out, moving further away from the heat, followed by more hot stuff rising up... A mushroom cloud makes it easier to see what's happening because its not just gas but also a lot of material too. so its much bigger and moves slower.fire is like that too but much smaller, faster, and more volatile and flickery as there is no material, just gas.

text: So, although air seems thin and can be moved easily, it still behaves like fluid in the sense that if something is lighter, or is less dense it rises up. text: When you breathe out when underwater, the air bubbles go up because air is less dense than water, and the air will rest on top of the water. It is exactly the same with a flame: hot air is less dense than cold air, so the heated air from the flame bubbles up, and carries the flame with it.

text:
The idea that women crave certain things during pregnancy for nutritional reasons has never been proven.What we do know is that hormones like estrogen and progesterone surge during pregnancy and this can dramatically alter how things taste and smell.So, foods women normally enjoy might not be appealing and ones they normally don’t like might become very appealing.There are also other hormone and chemical changes that boost appetite and general craving for food.
Another interesting thing is that it seems somewhat culture dependent.Western women might crave things like ice cream or Mac and cheese. Japanese women might crave rice or ramen. What is fairly common across cultures is that these tend to be comfort foods.
label: reasoning

text: Currently pregnant, southern woman here (in the heart of soul food country) and craving salads and vegetables, and completely avoiding anything heavy, sweet, or seasoned.We can look at overall trends and see how culture dictates them, but there's gotta be more to it than comfort. As you said, hormones change taste and smell, but aversions seem to go further than that. I can think of a food without smelling or tasting it and need to throw up. I'm sure that's related to the same mechanics, but I guess what I'm saying is there needs to be more research done. Like you said, nobody really knows.
label: analogy

text: Obviously anecdotal, but both times I was pregnant I knew cause I suddenly started craving tuna and I do not typically eat tuna. Omega-3 is great for fetal development so I feel like there is some sort of connection with cravings and things we need.

text: I wish more investigation would be done on this. I've found it so common for women to have an absolute revulsion to some foods seemingly overnight. text: Cravings get a lot of jokes on TV, but food aversions are more common in pregnancy.I have friends who ended up with hyperemesis gravidarium that caused them to throw up and renounce most foods to the point that they ended up in the hospital for dehydration.It’s hard to imagine our bodies deliberately causing nutritional deprivation to the point that it’s harmful to the mother and the baby. I’d wager it’s more chemical signaling in the body misfiring / going haywire. As another commenter said, when your hormones change, it impacts how foods taste. That just might be an evolutionary accident.

text: On a very anecdotal note, towards the end of my pregnancy with my oldest son, I could not abide creamy sauces. Think Alfredo sauce, even some Mac and cheese, they both made me want to throw up. I never had problems with those before. My now 24 year old son completely avoids rich creamy sauces. They make him gag.My co-worker experienced something similar with avocado. During both of her pregnancies she couldn’t eat it but afterwards it was fine. Both of her children refuse to eat avocado. They are not super picky eaters, but neither one will have anything to do with avocado.I don’t have enough evidence to suggest this as a legitimate theory, but it is pretty interesting.

text: Adderall is a dopamine reuptake inhibitor, so when cells release dopamine it allows it to swim around longer doing it's thing before getting pulled back into the cell.
label: reasoning

text: To expand on that: the reason this matters is because ADHD is essentially your brain looking for ways to be happy. You start to Do The Thing, you get a dopamine hit, then your brain sucks up the happy chemical and Doing The Thing no longer makes you happy, so your brain goes looking for a new Thing To Do to get more happychemical.Byy taking an uptake inhibitor, the happy chemical stays and Doing The Thing continues to make you happy, so you can continue to Do More Of The Thing without your brain nagging at you to Try That Different Thing.
label: analogy

text: Ritalian is a dopamine reuptake inhibitor. Adderall is an amphetamine

text: Motion sickness is caused when your brain receives conflicting information from the various parts of your body that sense movement.Your inner ears signal to your brain about acceleration and gravity; your eyes track visual movement; and your muscles, joints and inner organs (especially your guts) signal to your brain how each part of the body is shaped and moving relative to the rest of the body.When these signals all match up, the brain interprets this as everything being normal. But when these signals start to mismatch, it raises an alarm in the brain that something is very, very wrong.
label: reasoning
